sound_type: Deepest sustained note in nature -- blue whale 20Hz infrasonic pulse, at or below human hearing threshold, 180-188 dB source level, long-range ocean communication, E=5
ecosystem: Open Pacific -- deep ocean pelagic; NE Pacific range
season: Rare (NE Pacific; uncommon in nearshore PNW waters; population severely reduced by whaling)
milestone: SECOND BALAENOPTERIDAE at degree 279. Blue whale: largest animal in Earth's history (100-150 metric tons). 20 Hz infrasonic pulse: deepest sustained note in nature. E=5 because at/below human hearing threshold -- felt more than heard. Source level 180-188 dB; acoustically powerful but perceptually inaccessible to most humans. Balaenopteridae arc: humpback (278, E=9, maximum spectacle) to blue whale (279, E=5, maximum invisible depth). Combined E=13 with Seven Year Bitch (S36 279, E=8).

coordinates.description: NE Pacific open ocean. Blue whales are pelagic; occasional presence off Washington/Oregon coast in productive upwelling years. Primary habitat: open Pacific, including waters west of California and Pacific Northwest. Not regularly nearshore.

acoustic_data.frequency_hz: ~15-25 Hz primary vocalization; characteristic pulse at ~20 Hz. At or below human hearing threshold (~20 Hz lower limit for healthy young adults). Infrasonic for most adult human observers.
acoustic_data.source_level_db: ~180-188 dB re 1 µPa @ 1m (underwater acoustic reference). Among the loudest sustained biological sounds measured. Acoustically very powerful; perceptually inaccessible to most humans at this frequency.
acoustic_data.propagation: Low-frequency sound travels farther in water. Via SOFAR channel (Sound Fixing and Ranging), blue whale pulses may propagate hundreds to thousands of kilometers. Primary function: long-range contact/mate-finding call across vast open ocean.
acoustic_data.human_experience: At 20 Hz threshold frequency, perceived as physical sensation (pressure, vibration) rather than as sound. Felt before heard. The largest animal in history is experienced as a physical presence, not an acoustic event, by most human observers.
acoustic_data.e5_rationale: E=5 for experiential intensity, not source level. The sound has tremendous acoustic power (180-188 dB) but is at/below human perceptual threshold. Present, real, significant -- but not directly accessible. The paradox: largest animal, least audible signal.

nodes.title: Second Balaenopteridae -- The Arc
nodes.description: Degree 279 is the second Balaenopteridae entry. Humpback (278, E=9, spectacle) to blue whale (279, E=5, invisible depth). Same family, opposite registers. The arc spans maximum physical presence to maximum perceptual absence.

nodes.title: Largest Animal in Earth's History
nodes.description: Balaenoptera musculus: 100-150 metric tons, 24-30 meters. Exceeds any dinosaur. The largest biological mass in the fossil record across 3.8 billion years. Heart weighs ~600 kg (size of small car). Tongue weighs ~2.7 metric tons.

nodes.title: The Paradox: Largest Animal, Least Audible Signal
nodes.description: 20 Hz at/below human hearing threshold. E=5 for experiential inaccessibility despite 180-188 dB source level. The largest animal speaks in the register that most humans cannot hear. Felt more than heard. Physical presence without acoustic accessibility.

cross_references.relationship: First Balaenopteridae. The arc: E=9 maximum spectacle (humpback breach) to E=5 maximum invisible depth (blue whale pulse). Same family, opposite registers.
First Physeteridae. After the blue whale's infrasonic depth, the sperm whale enters with 230+ dB directional biosonar. Two great whale families in consecutive degrees.

Two acoustic extremes: orca clicks at 20-100 kHz (ultrasonic, above human hearing) vs. blue whale pulse at 20 Hz (infrasonic, below human hearing). Both beyond human perceptual range in opposite directions.
Combined E=13. Fierce, direct, audible (S36 E=8) vs. infrasonic, invisible, felt (SPS E=5). Both present; neither fully accessible.
